questions.question: What is the main idea behind secure defaults?
options: The easiest standard path should already be reasonably safe without custom heroics.
All features should be disabled forever.
Security settings should be chosen only after the first incident.
Every request should fail closed regardless of context.
questions.explanation: Secure defaults make the normal path safe enough that teams do not need risky custom workarounds.

questions.question: Why are permissive defaults dangerous in multitenant systems?
options: Because they quietly expand blast radius before operators notice.
Because they reduce CPU usage.
Because they remove the need for onboarding.
Because they only affect backups.
questions.explanation: Broad default access and missing limits make new mistakes dangerous immediately.

questions.question: Which default is the strongest baseline for new capabilities?
options: Deny by default, then grant explicit scoped access as needed.
Allow everything internally until a later audit.
Use one shared admin credential for faster launches.
Skip quotas for the first month.
questions.explanation: Explicit grants keep initial blast radius small.

questions.question: Why are expiring overrides better than indefinite emergency exceptions?
options: They force review and prevent temporary risk from silently becoming permanent policy drift.
They guarantee no incidents occur.
They eliminate the need for audit logs.
They remove all onboarding friction.
questions.explanation: Override expiry creates a natural cleanup point and review trigger.

questions.question: Which control most directly makes service onboarding safer by default?
options: Managed workload identity and short-lived credentials.
A wiki page that says 'be careful'.
Long-lived shared tokens stored in config files.
Manual host SSH for every deploy.
questions.explanation: Safe-by-default onboarding is strongest when the platform issues scoped ephemeral identity.

questions.question: What trade-off often comes with deny-by-default policy?
options: Lower initial blast radius but more explicit onboarding and policy-management work.
Guaranteed zero support load.
No need for audits or metrics.
Perfect usability for every edge case immediately.
questions.explanation: Safer defaults usually require clearer provisioning and policy workflows.

questions.question: Which metric best reveals that secure defaults are being bypassed?
options: Override count, override age, and shadow credential usage.
Only average response size.
Count of terminal tabs.
Markdown heading depth.
questions.explanation: Frequent or stale overrides often indicate the default path is not working well.

questions.question: Why should degraded-mode behavior be part of a secure-defaults answer?
options: Because defaults include how the system behaves when key controls like auth or challenge services are unhealthy.
Because degraded mode only matters for billing.
Because healthy systems never need fallback behavior.
Because it replaces incident response planning.
questions.explanation: Safe defaults also define what happens when security dependencies fail.
